rankings.writeup: Team Sean takes the top spot after their biggest win of the season — a 67-62 victory over Aron that featured dominant performances up and down the roster. Sean Lee went off for 29 points on 11-for-18 shooting, reminding everyone why he's one of the league's best scorers (23.2 PPG on 50.0% FG). But the story of this team remains Christian Kim, who is putting up historically absurd numbers: 18.8 PPG and 15.8 RPG on 64.4% shooting. His 18-rebound, 12-point outing against Aron included 6 offensive boards. Josh Paik added an 11-point, 11-rebound double-double of his own. At 3-1, Sean has the best record in the league and the roster depth to keep it.

Team Matt makes a massive leap after taking down the previously first-place Nathan squad, 57-54. Matt Paik led the charge with 24 points, 10 rebounds, 4 assists, and a block — his third straight game with double-digit rebounds. Kanghee Lee was arguably even more impactful with 16 points, 8 boards, and a season-high 4 steals. The defense was suffocating, holding Nathan to just 54 points — their lowest output of the season. At 2-1 with the league's best point differential per game (+2.0), this team is legit. The only concern: they've played just three games while everyone else has played four or five. More data needed, but the arrow is pointing straight up.

A two-game losing streak has Team Nathan tumbling from the top of the rankings. After the Week 3 blowout loss to Aron, they followed it up with a 57-54 defeat to Matt — marking the first time Nathan has lost back-to-back games in OBA history. Nathan Paik is still the league's best player by the numbers (27.6 PPG, 10.2 RPG, 3.2 APG), and his 26-point, 11-rebound, 5-three-pointer outing against Matt was impressive, but Austin Im struggled (4-for-20 from the field). When Austin gets going (he's still averaging 22.8 PPG with a league-best 48.6% from three), this team is elite. But the supporting cast needs to be more consistent — Ryan Choi went 1-for-8 against Matt. The talent is there for a bounce-back, but the margin for error is shrinking.

After the statement win over Nathan in Week 3, Team Aron came back down to earth with a 67-62 loss to Sean. Missing Jonathan Chung, Jon Han, and Brandon Kim forced them to rely on fill-in Andrew Paik, who delivered an impressive 16 points on 4-for-8 from three. Josh Sun continued his All-OBA campaign with 20 points, 8 rebounds, 6 assists, 4 steals, and 2 blocks — he's quietly averaging 18.5 PPG, 4.0 APG, and a league-best 2.8 SPG. Aron Kim chipped in 21 points and 10 rebounds. The biggest question for this team is consistency: they beat Nathan by 19 then lost to Sean by 5. When fully healthy, they have the firepower to beat anyone, but the 2-2 record tells the story of a team still searching for its identity.

Team PJo had the bye week, but the 0-4 record still looms large. The numbers paint a bleak picture: worst field goal percentage (32.0%), worst three-point percentage (19.3%), worst free throw percentage (47.6%), and a league-worst -9.2 point differential. The bright spots are individual — Wonjin Jang is averaging a double-double (16.7 PPG, 10.3 RPG) and PJo himself puts up 11.8 PPG and 11.0 RPG — but the team shooting has been abysmal. They need Joseph Yoo, who averaged 13.5 PPG in his two appearances, back in the lineup, and they desperately need someone to knock down threes (the team is shooting under 20% from deep). Every remaining game is must-win territory.
